Was I leaning foes? I leaning back? What was I even wearing? All those sort of things, when you're in person, they make just this incredible difference in your ability to get into rapport and your ability to establish trust. So let's go through some, you know, I want to go through on the whiteboard here, a very basic aspect of this, okay? And this is something you've heard many, many times before probably, it's this equation they say about, you know, there's 100% of communication, right? How human beings communicate with each other, right? And the point of this typically is that they say, well, you probably think the words, you know, are the bulk of communication. And when you actually test this out in the laboratory, and they've done so many studies, right? They typically get it to like where it's like about, you know, and I'm going to round it up here, like, you know, 45% of actual communication is tonality, 45% is body language, body language, right? And only 10% are the words that you say. And that's in line with my terrible fucking, hey, right, okay? So that, so the point of this is they try to tell you that this, you know, body language and tonality is just, you know, unconscious communication is power, okay, fucking deal, right? Yes. I think we all know this at this point that, you know, the words matter 100%. I mean, it's not that words don't matter. It don't matter just 10%. They matter 100%. That's not what this equation means. What this equation means is that while when you open up your mouth to speak, every word matters 100%. But we're constantly communicating without speaking. So when you're even though you might not be speaking, you're still communicating with someone. You're communicating intention, thought, feeling, time, everything. You're communicating all these things. And it happens mostly on an unconscious level. What that means is that they're not saying to themselves, hmm, this person is dressed this way. So that means he's trustworthy or he's a person of authority or an expert. It doesn't happen like that. It's simply, it's they look at you, they take it all in and it instantly bypasses the conscious mind, it goes straight to their gut to their unconscious, right? And which is in your brain actually, right? And they make a decision based on a pattern that they formed. What I mean by that is the way our brains work is our brains form patterns are unconscious minds. Your conscious mind has very limited processing power, maybe you know, a quality 3% of the world that you can actually take it. So right now, as I talk to you, right, what am I really focusing on? My focus is my conscious mind. I'm focusing on the camera right in front of me, right? I see Anjay sitting right behind the camera bowing to the left. I see the lights over here making the lighting purpose. So you can see me well, right? But what I'm not focusing on so much is the way the air feels on my skin or the background humble of the refrigerator over there. I'm not focusing on the fact that there's a light ray coming in. But now I am see, there's all this stuff that's happening around me, even how the chair feels on my legs as I push my gravity pushes me into the seat. I don't focus on that. Why? Because I can't. If I focused on all that shit, I go crazy. I don't my conscious mind, I'm a smart guy, right? But my conscious mind has a limited amount of processing power. So what happens is my brain is a certain part of your brain called the reticular activating system that decides that chooses what your conscious mind focuses on. So at any given moment, my reticular activating system is saying, okay, it's important to focus on the camera. It's important to focus on what you're going to say next on your own train of thought. It's important to focus maybe on that these I can't ignore certain stimuli like these lights are very bright. I can't ignore those, right? But it tells my conscious mind, don't worry about everything else. Don't worry, ignore it all because it's irrelevant right now to get the task done, the task at hand. This is what you should focus on. That's what your conscious mind, your reticular activating system does. So your conscious mind can't process everything, it's this much power, right? That being said, it's not like you lose all the other information. We would not be able to go through life like that because frankly, if a fucking like I played a practical joke on my on my staff last week, right? And on Halloween, I brought in this gigantic fucking 40 pound Python like a 12 foot Python, bro was fucking up on a chair, right? Anyway, the point is, is that if all of a sudden, so yeah, I'm focusing all the stuff, but my unconscious mind sees everything, every little bit of my field, every noise I'm not focusing on, it's all going to this unconscious mind, which is constantly on watch to keep me safe. It regulates my heartbeat, my breathing, my everything, everything's being regulated, all my autonomic reflexes are being made. And if something should come into my field of view, at any given moment, my unconscious mind will look at it and said, alert, fucking snake, danger, sends a signal to my conscious mind, like, what the fuck? And all of a sudden, I focus on that. That's how all, and this is really important. Usually I'm getting into this, it's important to understand, because what happens is when you're out there and selling and influencing, your prospect has only so much power in their conscious mind. So they focus on certain aspects of you. They focus on the words that you say, mostly, right? They focus on maybe some of that outstanding feature about you. They kind of got like, to go and sit on the space, right? There's something on your, the more, the more, the more, right? Okay. So they'll focus on one or two things, but everything else about you, how you're dressed, in the first second you walk in, yeah, they'll see that, but then they'll, immediately the brain does what's called habituates, meaning it sees something and it says, okay, no, it will eventually ignore it. Like when you first walk in, you get in a hot bath, it's fucking, you're like, oh, fuck it's so hot, right? Five minutes later, you think, oh, the water's just fine. That's not because the water cooled off. Let me degree, you got, your body gets, you're probably okay, it's safe, I'll delete out this information. It's not relevant. You get it? So what happens is, is the brain, the unconscious mind, in order to effectively manage you as you go through the world, it throughout your life, it's taken all this extra information that you've deleted and it forms unconscious patterns, patterns of behavior. So when I woke up to a door, okay, and it's a strange door and I've never seen the door before, I'm like, what the fuck, wait a second, I've never seen, I wonder, is it safe to go through this door? Is there a fucking, is there a different, the twilight zone outside? Is it gonna be like, I'll walk, it'll be a cliff outside, it can be wild animals waiting outside my door and, but here's what happens. I go to a strange door and my, as I approach my unconscious mind, it's, ah, okay, pattern for doors in non-combat areas where there's not gonna be fucking guns blazing, safe to go and I immediately, I don't have to go through every door and stop and analyze and say, what's this? What's this little knob here? It's a door, it's a fucking door because my brain hasn't made a pattern for going through doors in non-combatals. Promising this, drop me in Somalia during a war and I'm gonna open every door and peek around once because your brain, because I know I'm in a dangerous area, but the pattern for going through basic doors, right, that's already laid down in my mind and it's gonna be an automatic response, I'll walk through the door. It's why we can go through it without worrying about every crack in the sidewalk, you get it? Every new set of stairs we go through, I wonder if this leads to heaven, maybe? No, our brain knows that it's a pattern, stairs lead to where? The next floor, right? It's been laid down over entire lives, okay? The reason this is important is because when you're selling, when you're influencing, right, you need to be speaking to two minds at once, you're speaking to someone's logical mind, which is basically based on their conscious mind, it's a logical conscious mind and the unconscious mind, which we call the gut, you know, gut feelings, okay? And typically what happens is in the conscious mind, the influence occurs mostly through the words we say, through the creation of logical cases, erratite logical cases, through our words. Now, we then can apply certain tonalities to those words to give the words extra meaning, but those meanings that will pop up, if I say something to you, like, I say to you, hey, John, how you doing? You're like, I'm okay, I say, hey, John, how you doing today? And you're like, wow, he seems to really care. You heard those extra words, whether you knew it or not, your brain immediately applied extra meaning to my words, because of the tonality I used, right? That happened on a gut level, you can't control it. As soon as I say my words a certain way, it registers in you, which is why those tonalities are so crucial when you're out influencing, because you could add extra words on through your tonality. Well, guess what? The same thing happens with body language. And it's even more visceral with body language, especially when it comes to things like trust, respect, authority, those three things. Let me tell you something, one wrong body language move, and you're fucked, you really are, you not only can you not influence, people will destroy you, I'll give you an example, growing up, you might laugh at me, but my son had a good friend, his name was Lucas, right? Lucas was a nice, I gotta say, hopefully he's not watching, no, it's okay, it's all good, but when Lucas was growing up, he would not make eye contact with you, he was like eight or nine years old, his friend Lucas would come over and I'd say, hey, how you doing, Lucas? He's like, I'm okay, I'm like, Lucas, where are you? Hey, he wouldn't fucking make eye contact, and it made me distrust, and I distrusted the kid. Every time I try, hey, Lucas, how you doing? I'm okay, okay, Lucas, where are you? I'm like, Lucas, I look left, he looks, wouldn't make eye contact, right? And because that, I distrusted him. Think about all the times that you've been in a sales setting, where a salesman just said, you know, he wouldn't look you in the eye, there's something happen with that, and you just distrust him. Go back even further. When you were in school, you know, on the East Coast, they never had this, something called cooties, you know, and so, do you have cooties here in the East Coast? Okay, so when you're a kid, right, when you're really young, you know, oh, we're gonna get a cootie shot with him because against the muck, and when you had someone who had cooties as a kid, right, was it close to the words they said? No, it was how they what? How they looked, honestly, let's call it spades, spades, that's what it was. It was how they looked, how they walked, how they spit when they talk, okay, you need this fucking spit guard, right, how they were discombobulated in some way, the hair was a mess, they were just unkempt in some way, and those kids were considered to be like, no, I'm gonna cootie shot, now as we get old, we learn that's not nice, right, and we down-regulate our external response. But if you think for one fucking second that those patterns are not firmly laid down in you, and in every prospect that you see, you're kidding yourself. We judge books by their covers, we do, our parents did it, they said, don't judge a book by their cover, and they did it, and so do you. Our teachers said they did it, didn't we all do it. We judge books by their covers, it's a human thing, it's a survival mechanism that goes all the way back to the cave mandays, and that's why I always say in those first four seconds of a conversation, if you're not perceived the right way, you're fucked. Because what that chunks up to is that you're a person worth listening to, and higher level still, because you can help them achieve their goals, you can help them get what they want, that's what it chunks up to. If you're just thinking about this for a second, let's just really slow this down here because now we're about to get specific of what body language is inspiring that, and how do you avoid the pitfalls that cause that not to happen? Because let me tell you something, this is the key to the kingdom of getting into quick rapport is body language, and tonality when you're on the phone, but we'll get to that later, but the point is, think about it for a second, what do you want to see in someone to make them, because what would you expect someone who's sharp to look like, and act like, and sound like? What should they look like? What should they sound like? Someone who's on the ball, a born problem solver, someone who's not going to waste your time, because they don't want to waste their own time. Why? Because they're also experts in their field. What is an expert in your field? I don't know what you sell, okay, we can do some Q&A after we will, you can tell me what you sell, right? What do you sell? What should you look like? How should you dress? If you're a plumber, should you be wearing a three-piece suit to someone's door to fix their pipes? Probably not. They'll say, what the fuck is this? That's the best guy's going to charge me an arm and a leg. I got to pay for a suit, right? They'll say, well, it doesn't make sense. We have something called the law of congruency. You need to dress the way it's congruent with your profession. How would an expert in your field dress? How would they look? How would they carry themselves? Why is that the case? Because it's about those patterns. We've been conditioned, since we're yay big, to essentially accept certain things as the norm, and some things outside the norm. Some things were just visceral, like when a kid had the cooties, right? Well, you just knew it was off because it was outside the average. The same way they look at, they've tested, by language has been tested, by the way, in laboratories. They do all these tests, they show people a face, and they can tell based on the actual symmetry, how asymmetric someone's face is, the more asymmetric, the less trustworthy they're deemed. Seriously, that the more symmetrical and those, we, our brain has been conditioned, and there are certain natural responses that we have, where how someone looks, how someone carries themselves, how they dress, how they smell, how they move, what jewelry they wear, how they make eye contact, how they shake your hand, how far they stand from you, all these things. Do they stand directly in front of you? Do they stand off to the side? How do they move their hands when they're in front of you? What are their fingernails? What are their shoes look like? And by the way, just so you know, guys, women are fucking brutal with this stuff. Women are 10 times more observant than men when it comes to body language. Because what that chunks up to is that you're a person worth listening to who can help them achieve their goals, right? Then we must then go to the next step. It's okay. Well, the law of congruency states that, well, how should I dress? How should I act? How should I move? You're going to do that in a way that's congruent with the profession that you're in. How people expect an expert in your field to act and move and dress. How they would expect an expert in your field to be enthusiastic. You understand? If you're an actor in Hollywood, it's very different than someone who's a plumber in the field or electrician or a dentist. Now, this is, you know, if a dentist comes out, he's all fucking sales. He's like, what the fuck? So you have to be congruent. A dentist is not going to wear a suit. They wear a white dental smile. This is probably the most egregious violation of body language that you could ever make. And that people like home close talkers, okay, or space invaders like them, the game space, they're fucking space invaders, right. And typically along with space invading, they usually spitters as well. You want to get your fucking spit guard, that fucking like an umbrella, they're fucking spitting on you as they talk. Space invading is that one of the biggest faux pas, so to speak, in sales to get in someone's space bubble. Now, here's the deal in the Western world, outside of eight certain parts Asia, like Japan, China, okay, in the Western world, the space bubble was about two and a half to three feet. You do not want to get inside that bubble. Once you step inside that bubble, someone will feel uncomfortable. As soon as you go outside the bubble, they feel comfortable. Okay. Now, if you're in Asia, people tend to stand a bit closer together there. And if you notice why they've even been to Asia, you might feel uncomfortable. You're like, what the fuck? I'm that, I'm so cramped. And people will say it's cramped, but it's not that it's cramped. It's not like some, you know, whether they're a zillion people. So the reason you feel people always say, oh my God, it's so crowded there. It's not that it's crowded. There's as many people anywhere, right? Yeah, there's a billion people, but there's a lot of space. It's not that it's that they will get closer to you because it's their custom. And remember, this is about patterns of behavior. Okay. Next. Okay. Let's go through what's called wrapping your package. All right. Meaning how do you dress? You know, the basic premise that we have is like dressing for success. But that, I think that's a misconception because, you know, it's really not about this. I call dressing for congruency, dressing for what's appropriate. In other words, in some parts of the world and in certain professions, you know, you dress one way and other parts of the world you dress another. So it's not like just, you know, because dressing for success almost like it makes you think, well, like a suit and tie almost, right? And it makes you like dress your success. I got to get looked really good. It's not that. Okay. Dressing for success means dressing to close, dressing to close sales, dressing to influence, dressing to persuade. And that means you need to dress how an expert is expected to dress in your field. Let me give you an example. I go back to the plumber again, right? So with the plumber, what the fuck was that? Okay. Okay. It's fine. So with the plumber, right? So how should a plumber, an electrician, how should someone like more of a labor type, right? Patch on the, over here over the chest, his name on the patch, the company's name above that. Nice, okay. Clean work shoes, not dirty and disgusting. Belt with his tools on there or whatever, holding a clipboard in his hand when he comes in your door, right? Or you're with the thing holding a clipboard. How's that? Right? That, that's, you say, well, okay, this guy looks like he's going to come in there and fix my pipes and do a nice clean job. He'll do a great job, right? Well, what if the same guy came in and he had his pants sagging down to his ass cracks. He had plumbers butt. He had a disgusting shirt with paint, you know, splatter on it and he himself was unkempt and dirty. What would you think of that guy? Well, you might not throw him out of your house if you need your pipes fixed, right? But, you know, in the world of plumbing, there's massive sales opportunities. In other words, the plumbing world is typically based on emergency calls. You don't like call plumbers and hey, you want to just come in, do a normal check here, make sure my pipes are clean. Double in time for that, right? And the point is, is that, you know, you don't, I couldn't help that, right? You don't call a plumber, make sure your pipes are clean. You go to like fucking four and up, okay? Anyway, that's true. Anyway, the point is, is that the world of plumbing electricity, electricians, right, laborers, not as much construction, mostly plumbing, electricity, right? It's a lot of emergency calls. Might have a flood in the house, things aren't working, and you call up an emergency number, they send over the plumber or the electrician, and he comes in, he fixes something, right? Well, guess what? You know, I was hired by a plumbing company a bunch of years back, and they wanted me to help increase their sales, right? So we came up with a program for them, where essentially a plumber would come in, they get an emergency call, and a plumber would come in, and rather than just going in and fixing the thing and leaving, right, he'd say, oh, you know, as long as we want to do a free, you know, health check of your entire pipes and everything, right? And you're, like, you're having to get, say, yeah, of course, it's free, right? And the guy would come in, and variably, of course, right? If you check someone's pipes around the house, they'll, like, you know, the sinks in the bathroom, you're going to find problems, corrosion leaks, things that are about to break, right? And what they found is about 80% of the time, they're able to turn an $80 emergency call, where they actually lose money on the call, and turn it to a $1,000 sale by doing this free survey around the house, right? What happened was, you found out pretty quickly that some of the plumbers were really good at it, the ones that actually knew how to sell would do great, and those that weren't so good at it would have, would struggle, right? And what it came down to is the ones that knew how to sell would come in, they would look good, they would act, they knew all the secrets to body language, basic persuasion, right? So I started retraining them, and that worked as well, too. We trained people, and had them wear their nice clothing, right? You know, the right, you know, patch, I think, you know, clean cut, show them how to make eye contact, and that worked. Worked so well, in fact, that the company almost blew itself up, because what happened was, is every single job turned into like a long, so a one-hour emergency call turned into like a three-day job always, right? So they had no more plumbers out there to answer emergency calls, they started getting calls that couldn't answer, sorry, your gym is backed up now, but they got back, they couldn't grow their business anymore. So we had to restructure it so that we would actually do the health check and say, you know what, we'll fix the emergency now, and we can, we can actually do the whole, we can actually do the whole, all the work now, or if you let us come back on a schedule call next week, we'll give you a 30% discount. They'll say, oh, I'll take the discount, and you started scheduling in the, all these extra jobs you picked up, and having, so what we did is then we took the ones that were really natural at sales and were doing well, we made those the ones that would do your emergency calls. So the emergency calls were handled by someone who actually was a great salesperson, and they would do the survey, and then sell them on a much bigger job, right? And then for the actual work that came with these jobs, bigger jobs, you'd send the guys that really weren't that great at selling, and they'd actually do the manual labor. You get it? So, so don't, when I say plumbers, that's a sales, you can really make sales as a plumber, any contractor, if you know how to do it. Key is, is that what we found the big thing was, is that when they showed up with the clipboard, the name patch, clean cut, tool belt, hat, company hat, with the name on it, sales spiked, and they had a lot less problem actually closing on those bigger jobs. Before that training, you had some that were naturally good and a lot that weren't, okay? So the point is, is for a plumber or any manual labor, it's not about a suit and tie, it's about dressing the way you expect someone to dress. Here's another example, let's say you're a casino host, seriously, and a cas, the job of a casino host is to what? Attract high roles, make sure they feel good when they're there, right? How should that person dress? Well, they should probably just in a really snazzy snappy suit with a spread collar, a thin tie, maybe a diamond pinky ring, right? So what, if let's say you allow a financial advisor, how should you dress? How you doing? Equal pressure, right? Nice firm handshake. And if you want to add a bit of warmth, how you doing? Okay? Now, they say this is aggressive, like when Donald Trump goes like he goes, this, how you doing? They say it's not aggressive. It's more trying to display warmth and feeling, like more rapport. So, you great? Hey, buddy, how you doing here? How do you feel? Comfortable, right? So, I, if I, if I want to show some extra warmth, it's how you doing? Nice to meet you. And I like this, all the way down here, not too much, it's enough to know. Not wanting to be like, hey, how you doing? I'm screaming. You ever had that with someone else who was supposed to massage you? You're like, what the fuck? I had friends of guys that would do it. They would fucking rub you and fucking give you a massage. They're like, oh, nice to meet you, right? Okay? So, again, that's called your basic cooperated handshake. Then, you have the aggressive, which is how you doing? Like, damn, glad to. And you're like, what the fuck does that guy have to prove? And it turns people off. And then you have the worst one of all, which is a limp, how you doing? Nice to, you know, and by the way, thanks. That's that, by the way, interestingly enough, that last one that like that limp, that's almost your ultimate power handshake. We were not, it's like the one saying, I don't give a shit about you. Like, I'm so above you. I'm not even expend the energy it takes to shake your fucking hand. Believe it or not, that's what that means. So, remember, to me, I play it safe. I always use the basic cooperators handshake, which is straight up, not like this, not like that, even pressure. So return that pressure in kind, make sure there's a minimum pressure. Now, I'm going to close with this thought and we'll go to our Q&A, okay? Is that sometimes if someone sits like this, it's not because they're closed, they're fucking cold. It's not the way someone, no, obviously I'd rather have someone like this than like this, but it's not the, it's much more subtle than that. Like that's like the lowest level of body language. The point is, is that you can, close this thought here, is that you can change someone else's body language by using your own body language, something called pacing and leading, which I'll get into next with tonality when we do tonality next. But pacing and leading basically means that you can, you know, you enter someone's role there. So, if someone is sitting like this, then you want to basically adopt the same physiologies then. And then you sit there, and then you open up yourself and they'll follow you. So, you enter someone, so someone's like this and you want to be more open, then you would actually start, and you sit like this, like shoulder hearts, I can't do it, right? So, you sit like this and then you, you, uh-huh, yep, and then if you open up, okay, then they will tend to open up. Remember, you want to adopt someone else's physiology as much as possible. They're sitting a certain way, then you sit a certain way, okay? But eventually you want to have them follow you. So, if you enter their role, they are, then you pace them, you say, you stay that way, then you slowly move in the direction you want to go. That's the key. But don't get so close.
